summary: Simon Späti argues that conversational BI powered by Model Context Protocol (MCP) finally delivers on the two-decade-old promise of self-serve business intelligence. Unlike traditional BI where self-serve meant filtering dashboards, MCP enables LLMs to autonomously query databases, verify facts, and generate BI artifacts through natural language prompts. The post identifies three distinct output types—numerical data retrieval, AI-generated text interpretations, and visual charts—that combine to create a more powerful workflow than either humans or AI alone. Späti emphasizes that narrowing the analytical context makes AI more deterministic and less prone to hallucination, while semantic layers provide the crucial business context LLMs need to understand domain-specific metrics.
key_points: MCP standardizes AI integration with data tools the way LSP standardized language servers for IDEs, enabling plug-and-play connectivity between any AI model and BI systems
Conversational BI produces three distinct output types: numerical data (factual, non-hallucinated), text interpretations (AI-powered insights), and visuals (for human verification and exploration)
Narrowing the analytical context—focusing on specific data points rather than broad dashboards—makes AI responses more deterministic and accurate
The combination of BI-as-Code, semantic metrics layers, and real-time databases creates the foundation for effective human-AI collaboration
Domain experts remain essential as human oversight ensures AI-generated insights align with business reality and catch potential errors
money_quotes: With the right setup, conversations can generate BI artifacts.
Domain experts know the business and numbers best, so they are also the best ones to check the numbers and iteratively create BI artifacts.
Many data warehouses or data lakes aren't fast enough to support conversation-speed interrogations.
By going narrower, like analyzing a spike in a dashboard, it's much less likely to hallucinate and makes its prediction more deterministic.
Conversational BI is self-serve, too, but it is slower and more imprecise in a grand context, though super helpful in a narrow context.
The key insight from our exploration of conversational BI is fundamental: conversations can generate code, and code generates insights.
Conversational BI finally delivers on the promise of self-serve BI that the industry has pursued for two decades.
key_insight: True self-serve BI becomes possible when LLMs can autonomously query semantic layers through MCP, but effectiveness depends on narrowing analytical context and maintaining human oversight from domain experts.
